concepts.title: Battery and EMF
concepts.conceptBody:
Battery and EMF 

Suppose a resistance $\left( R \right)$ is connected across the terminals of a battery. A potential difference is developed across its ends. Current (or positive charge) flows from higher potential to lower potential across the resistance by itself. But inside the battery work has to be done to bring the positive charge from lower potential to higher potential. The influence that makes current flow from lower to higher potential (inside the battery) is called electromotive force (abbreviated emf). If $\text{W}$ work is done by the battery in taking a charge $q$ from negative terminal to positive terminal, then work done by the battery per unit charge is called emf $\left( \text{E} \right)$ of the battery.

 

Thus, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ E}=\frac{\text{W}}{\text{q}}$

The name electromotive force is misleading in the sense that emf is not a force it is work done per unit charge. The SI unit of emf is $\text{J}/\text{C}$ or $\text{V}\left( 1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}=1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ J}/\text{C} \right)$.

Important Points to Remember 

 

1.A battery is a device which maintains a potential difference between its two terminals. It is often prepared by putting two rods or plates of different metals in a chemical solution. Some internal mechanism 
exerts forces $\left( {{{\vec{F}}}_{n}} \right)$ on the ions (positive and negative) of the solution. This force drives positive ions towards positive terminal and negative ions towards negative terminal. As positive charge accumulates on anode and negative charge on cathode a potential difference and hence an electric field $\vec{E}$ is developed from anode to cathode. This electric field exerts an electrostatic force ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{e}}}=\text{q}\vec{E}$ on the ions. This force is opposite to that of ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{n}}}$. In equilibrium (steady state)

${{\text{F}}_{\text{n}}}={{\text{F}}_{\text{e}}}$

and no further accumulation of charge takes place.

2.The emf is the work done per unit charge by the battery force ${{F}_{n}}$ which is non electrostatic in nature.

3.When the terminals of the battery are connected by a conducting wire, an electric field is developed in the wire in the direction shown in Fig. The free electrons in the wire move in the opposite direction and enter the battery at positive terminal. Some electrons are withdrawn from the negative terminal. Thus, potential difference and hence, ${{\text{F}}_{\text{e}}}$ decreases in magnitude while ${{\text{F}}_{\text{n}}}$ remains the same. Thus, there is a net force on the positive charge towards the positive terminal. With this the positive charge rush towards positive terminal and negative charge rush towards negative terminal. Thus, the potential difference between positive and negative terminal is maintained.

4.During the charging of a battery current is driven into a battery in the reverse direction. In such a case positive charge enters the battery at the positive terminal and leaves the battery from the negative terminal.

5.In an open circuit the electrostatic force ${{\vec{F}}_{e}}$ and non-electrostatic force ${{\vec{F}}_{n}}$ on a charge $q$ are equal.

6.The work done by ${{\vec{F}}_{n}}$ on a positive charge $q$ moving from negative terminal to positive terminal is $\text{qE}$, where $\text{E}$ is the emf of the battery.

concepts.title: Potential Difference Across the Terminals of Battery
concepts.conceptBody:
Internal Resistance (r) and Potential Difference (V) across the terminals of a Battery 

The potential difference across a real source in a circuit is not equal to the emf of the cell. The reason is that charge moving through the electrolyte of the cell encounters resistance. We call this the internal resistance of the source, denoted by $r$. If this resistance behaves according to Ohm's law $r$ is constant and independent of the current i. As the current moves through $r$, it experiences an associated drops in potential equal to ir. Thus, when a current is drawn through a source, the potential difference between the terminals of the source is,

$\text{V}=\text{E}-\text{ir}$

This can also be shown as below:

${{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}-\text{E}+\text{ir}$ $~={{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}$

Or ${{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}$ $~=\text{E}-\text{ir}$

Following three special cases are possible:

(i) If the current flows in opposite direction (as in case of charging of a battery), then $\text{V}=\text{E}+\text{ir}$.

(ii) $\text{V}=\text{E}$, if the current through the cell is zero.

(iii) $\text{V}=0$, if the cell is short circuited.

This is because current in the circuit

$i=\frac{E}{r}$

Or E = ir

$\therefore $ E – ir = 0

Or V = 0

Thus, we can summaries it as follows:

concepts.title: Maximum Power Transfer Theorem
concepts.conceptBody:
Maximum Power Transfer Theorem 

"For maximum power (i.e. maximum current) being dissipated through an external load, connected in a circuit with battery of internal resistance $r$, the external resistance must be equal to internal resistance of the battery."

 

Derivation: 

$\text{i}=\frac{\varepsilon }{\left( \text{R}+\text{r} \right)}$

$\therefore $ $~\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P}={{\text{i}}^{2}}\text{R}=\frac{{{\varepsilon }^{2}}\text{R}}{{{(\text{R}+\text{r})}^{2}}}$ 

 

Differentiate P wrt R,

$\left( \frac{dP}{dR} \right)$$~=\frac{{{\varepsilon }^{2}}{{(R+r)}^{2}}-{{\varepsilon }^{2}}R\left[ 2\left( R+r \right) \right]}{{{(R+r)}^{4}}}$

$~=\frac{\left. {{\varepsilon }^{2}}\left[ {{R}^{2}}+{{r}^{2}}+2Rr-2{{R}^{2}}-2rR \right) \right]}{{{(R+r)}^{4}}}$

$~=\frac{{{\varepsilon }^{2}}\left( r-R \right)}{{{(R+r)}^{3}}}$

Hence, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \frac{\text{d}P}{\text{dR}} \right)=0$, when $\left( \text{r}=\text{R} \right)$

Hence power dissipated is maximum when $\left( r=R \right)$.

concepts.title: Series Grouping of Identical Cells
concepts.conceptBody:
Series Grouping of Identical Cells 

 

$\varepsilon $ is the emf of each cell and $\text{r}$ is the internal resistance

$\text{I}=\frac{\text{n}\varepsilon }{\left( \text{R}+\text{nr} \right)}$

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {{\varepsilon }_{\text{eq}}} & ~=\left( \text{n}\varepsilon \right) \\ \text{Req} & ~=\left( \text{nr} \right) \\ \end{array}$

When $\text{R}>>\text{r}$,

$\text{I}=\left( \frac{\text{n}\varepsilon }{\text{R}} \right)$

$\therefore $ The series grouping is ideal when $\text{R}>>\text{r}$.

concepts.title: Parallel Grouping of Identical Cells
concepts.conceptBody:
Parallel Grouping of Identical Cells 

  

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\frac{\varepsilon }{R+\left( \frac{r}{n} \right)}=\frac{n\varepsilon }{\left( nR+r \right)}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\varepsilon }_{\text{eq }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\varepsilon $, Req $=\left( \frac{r}{n} \right)$

When $\text{r}>>\text{R}$

$\text{I}=\left( \frac{\text{n}\varepsilon }{\text{r}} \right)$

 

Hence parallel grouping becomes effective when $(\text{R}<<\text{r})$.

concepts.title: Mixed Grouping of Identical Cells
concepts.conceptBody:
Mixed grouping 

$\text{n}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ no}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ cells }\!\!~\!\!\text{ in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ each }\!\!~\!\!\text{ row}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$\text{m}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ no}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ row }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$~\therefore $ $I=\frac{n\varepsilon }{R+\left( \frac{nr}{m} \right)}$

$I=\frac{mn\varepsilon }{\left( mR+nr \right)}$ 

Combination for maximum current 

It is maximum when $\left( \text{mR}+\text{nr} \right)$ is minimum

$\left( \text{mR}+\text{nr} \right)$ $~={{(\sqrt{\text{mR}})}^{2}}+{{(\sqrt{\text{nr}})}^{2}}$

$~={{(\sqrt{\text{mR}}-\sqrt{\text{nr}})}^{2}}+\sqrt{4\text{mnrR}}$

$~={{(\sqrt{\text{mR}}-\sqrt{\text{nr}})}^{2}}+2\sqrt{\text{mnrR}}$

$\therefore $ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{mR}+\text{nr} \right)\text{min}$ $~=2\sqrt{\text{mnrR}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ when }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\sqrt{\text{mR}}=\sqrt{\text{nr}}$

For maximum current output through load

$\begin{matrix}
\text{mR}=\text{nr} \\
\text{I}=\frac{\text{mn}\varepsilon }{\text{mR}+\text{nr}}=\frac{\text{mn}\varepsilon }{2\text{mR}}=\frac{\text{n}\varepsilon }{2\text{R}}=\left( \frac{\text{m}\varepsilon }{2\text{r}} \right) \\
\end{matrix}$

concepts.title: Series Grouping of Cells
concepts.conceptBody:
Series Grouping of Cells 

 

\(\begin{aligned} & I=\frac{\left(\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2 \ldots \ldots \varepsilon_n\right)}{R+\left(r_1+r_2+\ldots r_n\right)} \\ & I=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n \varepsilon_i}{R+\sum_i^n r_i} \\ & \varepsilon_{e q}=\sum^n{ }_{i=1} \varepsilon_i \\ & \text {Req. }=\sum^n{ }_{i=1} r_i\end{aligned}\)

concepts.title: Parallel Grouping of Cells
concepts.conceptBody:
Parallel Grouping of Cells 

 

First we will derive for two cells, then we will generalize the result.

$I=\left(i_1+i_2\right)$

$\mathrm{i}_1=\frac{(\varepsilon-\mathrm{IR})}{\mathrm{r}_1} ; \mathrm{i}_2=\left(\frac{\varepsilon-\mathrm{IR}}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)$

$\therefore $ $\therefore \mathrm{I}=\left(\frac{\varepsilon-\mathrm{IR}}{\mathrm{r}_1}\right)+\left(\frac{\varepsilon-\mathrm{IR}}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)$

$=\mathrm{I}\left[1+\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right]=\left(\frac{\varepsilon_1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)$

$\therefore $ $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\left[\frac{\varepsilon_1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)+1\right]}=\frac{\left[\left(\frac{\varepsilon_1}{\mathrm{r}_1}\right)+\left(\frac{\varepsilon_2}{\mathrm{n}}\right)\right]\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right]^{-1}}{\left[\mathrm{R}+\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)^{-1}\right]}$

$\therefore $ $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\left[\left(\frac{\varepsilon_1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{\varepsilon_2}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)^{-1}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{R}+\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)^{-1}\right]}$

In general,

$\text{I}=\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Sigma\!\!\text{ }\left( \frac{{{\varepsilon }_{\text{i}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{i}}}} \right){{\left[ \text{ }\!\!\Sigma\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{i}}}} \right]}^{-1}}}{\text{R}+{{\left( \text{ }\!\!\Sigma\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{i}}}} \right)}^{-1}}}$

or, $\text{I}=\frac{\left[ \text{ }\!\!\Sigma\!\!\text{ }\left( \frac{{{\varepsilon }_{\text{i}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{i}}}} \right) \right]{{\text{r}}_{\text{eq}}}}{\text{R}+\left( {{\text{r}}_{\text{eq}}} \right)}$ where $\left( \frac{1}{{{r}_{\text{eq }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}=\frac{1}{{{r}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{r}_{2}}}+\frac{1}{{{r}_{3}}}+\ldots \cdot \frac{1}{{{r}_{n}}} \right)$
